danger_signals.visual_form: Rage meter fills from damage taken, visual indicator on HUD
danger_signals.meaning: Player/opponent in heightened aggressive state; increased combat threat level

danger_signals.visual_form: Blade-to-blade contact with visual impact effect
danger_signals.meaning: Direct combat engagement; immediate danger from opponent
Imminent melee threat; high-pressure combat state

danger_signals.visual_form: Character knockback, screen shake, health bar depletion
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat inflicting harm; opponent has successful strike

safety_signals.visual_form: Increased space between fighters on screen
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary reprieve from immediate melee threat; opportunity to recover

safety_signals.visual_form: Character in relaxed/ready posture, no active combo animations
Referee figure present in background, fighters positioned at screen edges
safety_signals.meaning: Formal combat beginning; controlled arena environment

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Traditional Japanese weapon designs (katana, naginata); period clothing
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Foreign-born characters visually distinct through non-Japanese armor/weapons
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: 18th-century Japanese setting; traditional aesthetic in stage backgrounds

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Non-traditional weapons; Western or exotic armor designs
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Characters explicitly designed with foreign visual markers to distinguish from native Japanese warriors
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Presence in historical Japan setting creates implicit narrative tension (Sakoku period context)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Dramatically different silhouettes and animation sets compared to human fighters
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Anachronistic presence in historical setting signals supernatural threat level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Traditional Japanese aesthetic in character designs, weapons, clothing, and stage environments; use of Japanese calligraphy style in character art
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters speak exclusively in Japanese with period-appropriate dialects ranging from archaic formality to contemporary slang; combat reflects samurai martial traditions

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Cultural collision (foreign presence in isolated Japan)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Juxtaposition of foreign-born characters with distinctly non-Japanese visual designs against Japanese samurai aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Multiple character nationalities represented through design and voice; creates implicit political/cultural tension through roster diversity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rage gauge visual indicator increases proportionally with damage received; character animation becomes more aggressive/frantic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Rage activation enables enhanced combat capabilities; damaged fighters gain mechanical advantage through accumulated pain/anger

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Referee figure present in background during match; fighters begin/end at designated screen positions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Structured, rule-bound combat; matches follow formal tournament/duel conventions rather than chaotic fighting

environmental_storytelling.example: Real historical figures (Hattori Hanzō, Yagyū Jūbei) maintain accurate names and period appearance, establishing setting authenticity and Japanese cultural specificity
Traditional Japanese weapons (katana, naginata) versus foreign weapon types visually communicate character origin and allegiance without explicit exposition
Heavy incorporation of traditional Japanese instruments (shakuhachi, shamisen, koto, taiko) and later enka creates immersive 18th-century Japanese soundscape
Dialogue exclusively in Japanese with varying dialects (archaic formality to modern slang) grounds world in specific cultural/temporal context; maintained in international releases
Background referee figure establishes combat legitimacy and formal ruleset; implies societal/political structure governing warrior conflicts

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Knockback animation, health bar depletion, body flinch responses trigger Rage gauge increase
Enhanced attack capabilities enabled; character animation becomes more aggressive; mechanical advantage gained from accumulated damage state
Neutral stance, weapon drawn, attention focused on opponent; prepared for incoming threat
Visual distinctiveness communicates outsider status within Japanese cultural context; implicit narrative tension through mere presence during Sakoku period
Non-human animation patterns; fantastical move effects distinguish from martial realism of human fighters; signals anachronistic/otherworldly threat

notes: Samurai Shodown SNES is a port of arcade original. The Two Fires Manifestation Layer can leverage: (1) Rage gauge as dynamic world-state visual feedback mechanism tied to character injury/emotional state; (2) Character design taxonomy (Japanese native vs. foreign vs. supernatural) as faction communication without explicit labels; (3) Language/music integration as diegetic world-building; (4) Referee presence as institutional authority signal. Limited direct narrative/political exposition means world-state communication is primarily through aesthetic consistency and mechanical metaphor. The Rage system particularly exemplifies state manifestation—visible accumulation of player damage directly manifests as mechanical/visual combat advantage, making internal emotional state externally readable.
sources_used: tcrf - Samurai Shodown (SNES) - The Cutting Room Floor
